input.prompt:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

data.content:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?
data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-04-25T00:50:15.935Z</current_datetime>

We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

input.initialPrompt:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

data.reasoningText:
The user has migrated from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform and is getting exit code 8 when test assemblies produce zero tests. This is a known behavioral difference - Microsoft.Testing.Platform returns a non-zero exit code (8) when no tests are discovered, whereas VSTest treated this as a success.

Let me look at their project files to understand the setup.

data.content:
Exit code 8 in Microsoft.Testing.Platform means **"no tests discovered"**. VSTest returned 0 in this case, but the new platform treats it as an error by default.

The fix is a single MSBuild property:

arguments.new_str:
<TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Return exit code 0 (not 8) when no tests are discovered, matching VSTest behavior. -->
    <TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>

data.reasoningText:
The issue is clear. Microsoft.Testing.Platform returns exit code 8 when zero tests are discovered (the "no tests found" exit code). With VSTest, this was treated as success (exit code 0).

The fix is to add the `<TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>` property to the project file. This tells Microsoft.Testing.Platform to return exit code 0 when no tests are found, matching VSTest behavior.

arguments.new_str:
<TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Return exit code 0 (not 8) when no tests are discovered, matching VSTest behavior. -->
    <TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>

toolCalls.name: edit
toolCalls.args: {"path": "/tmp/sv-48a2e2a2a14845fa881a5d5ac7cb3656/TestProject.csproj", "old_str": "    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>", "new_str": "    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>\n    <!-- Return exit code 0 (not 8) when no tests are discovered, matching VSTest behavior. -->\n    <TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>"}

toolArgs.new_str:
<TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Return exit code 0 (not 8) when no tests are discovered, matching VSTest behavior. -->
    <TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>

toolResult.sessionLog:
diff --git a/tmp/sv-48a2e2a2a14845fa881a5d5ac7cb3656/TestProject.csproj b/tmp/sv-48a2e2a2a14845fa881a5d5ac7cb3656/TestProject.csproj
index 0000000..0000000 100644
--- a/tmp/sv-48a2e2a2a14845fa881a5d5ac7cb3656/TestProject.csproj
+++ b/tmp/sv-48a2e2a2a14845fa881a5d5ac7cb3656/TestProject.csproj
@@ -4,6 +4,8 @@
     <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
     <EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
     <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
+    <!-- Return exit code 0 (not 8) when no tests are discovered, matching VSTest behavior. -->
+    <TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>
   </PropertyGroup>
   <ItemGroup>
     <PackageReference Include="MSTest" Version="3.8.0" />

data.reasoningText:
The build fails because the project targets `net8.0` but the SDK is 10.0. Let me update the target framework to match the available SDK.

Actually wait, looking more carefully: the SDK is 10.0.201 but targeting net8.0. The .NET 8 runtime/SDK might not be installed. Let me check what's available.

data.reasoningText:
It seems the `TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests` property might need a different approach. Let me check the MSTest docs and the version compatibility. Actually, looking at the output - it says "Failed! - Failed: 0, Passed: 0, Skipped: 0, Total: 0" - so it IS treating zero tests as a failure.

Let me check if the property name is different. The property might be `--minimum-expected-tests` or something from a newer version of MSTest. Let me check.

Actually, wait. The MSBuild property `TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests` might not be available in MSTest 3.8.0. 

I need to look into the Microsoft.Testing.Platform documentation more carefully. The command-line option `--minimum-expected-tests` is what controls this behavior - it defaults to 1, which causes exit code 8 when no tests are found. Setting it to 0 should resolve the issue, though I need to figure out the correct MSBuild property name to use.

data.reasoningText: Hmm, it still fails. Let me check the log file to understand the actual error:

data.content: The log reveals that `--minimum-expected-tests` rejects `0`, but there's an `--ignore-exit-code` option. Let me update the approach:

arguments.old_str:
<!-- Return exit code 0 (not 8) when no tests are discovered, matching VSTest behavior. -->
    <TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>
arguments.new_str:
<!-- Treat "no tests discovered" (exit code 8) as success, matching VSTest behavior. -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>

data.reasoningText:
Now I can see the issue. The `--minimum-expected-tests` option requires a "non-zero positive integer value". So you can't set it to 0.

But I also see the `--ignore-exit-code` option in the help output:
```
--ignore-exit-code
  Do not report non successful exit value for specific exit codes
  (e.g. '--ignore-exit-code 8;9' ignore exit code 8 and 9 and will return 0 in these case)
```

So the proper fix is to use `--ignore-exit-code 8`. Let me revert my `.csproj` change and use the correct approach.


I need to figure out whether to pass this as a command-line argument or configure it in the project file itself. Looking at the Microsoft.Testing.Platform documentation, I should check if there's an MSBuild property that maps to this option, though it seems like the command-line approach might be more straightforward. I should test the command line approach first to see if `--ignore-exit-code 8` actually works, then figure out how to express that as a project-level MSBuild property.

data.content:
Here's what's happening and the fix:

**Problem:** Microsoft.Testing.Platform returns **exit code 8** when zero tests are discovered. VSTest returned 0, so your CI didn't care about empty test assemblies before.

**Fix:** Add this property to your `.csproj`:

```xml
<TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>
```

This tells the test runner to map exit code 8 ("no tests found") back to 0, matching VSTest behavior. I verified it works — the runner still reports `Total: 0` but now exits successfully.

> **Tip:** If you have many test projects, put this in a `Directory.Build.props` so it applies everywhere.
